{{Short description="Phenomenon in which two waves superpose to form a resultant wave"}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Interference
| image = Interference of water waves with obstacles.png
| caption = Water wave interference: overlapping circular waves produce regions of constructive and destructive interference.
| topics = [[Superposition principle]], [[Constructive interference]], [[Destructive interference]], [[Young's double-slit experiment]], [[Thin-film interference]]
}}

In [[physics]], '''interference''' is a phenomenon in which two [[wave]]s superpose to form a resultant wave of greater, lower, or the same [[amplitude]]. Interference usually refers to the interaction of waves that are correlated or [[coherence (physics)|coherent]] with each other, either because they come from the same source or because they have the same or nearly the same [[frequency]].

Interference effects can be observed with all types of waves, including [[light]], [[radio wave|radio]], [[sound wave|acoustic]], [[surface water wave]]s, [[gravity wave]]s, or [[matter wave]]s.

== Superposition principle ==
{{Main|Superposition principle}}
When two or more waves overlap, the resultant displacement equals the algebraic sum of the individual displacements:
:<math>y(x,t) = y_1(x,t) + y_2(x,t)</math>

== Constructive and destructive interference ==

=== Constructive interference ===
When waves are in [[phase (waves)|phase]] (phase difference = 0, 2π, 4π, …):
:<math>A_{\text{resultant}} = A_1 + A_2</math>

For identical amplitudes: <math>I \propto (A_1 + A_2)^2 = 4A^2</math>

=== Destructive interference ===
When waves are out of phase by π (180°):
:<math>A_{\text{resultant}} = |A_1 - A_2|</math>

For identical amplitudes: complete cancellation (<math>I = 0</math>).

== Young's double-slit experiment ==
{{Main|Young's interference experiment}}
[[Thomas Young (scientist)|Thomas Young]] demonstrated light interference in 1801, providing decisive evidence for the [[wave theory of light]].

Bright fringes occur at:
:<math>d\sin\theta = m\lambda, \quad m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots</math>

Dark fringes occur at:
:<math>d\sin\theta = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda, \quad m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots</math>

Fringe spacing on a screen at distance <math>L</math>:
:<math>\Delta y = \frac{\lambda L}{d}</math>

== Thin-film interference ==
{{Main|Thin-film interference}}
Light reflecting from the top and bottom surfaces of a thin film can interfere. For normal incidence in a film of thickness <math>t</math> and refractive index <math>n</math>:

Constructive (in air, with no phase reversal at one surface):
:<math>2nt = \left(m + \frac{1}{2}\right)\lambda, \quad m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots</math>

Examples: [[soap bubble]]s, [[oil slick]]s, anti-reflection coatings, [[Newton's rings]].

== Michelson interferometer ==
{{Main|Michelson interferometer}}
Splits a beam into two paths, recombines them. Fringe shift <math>\Delta N</math> when one mirror moves by <math>\Delta d</math>:
:<math>\Delta d = \frac{\Delta N \cdot \lambda}{2}</math>

Historically used in the [[Michelson–Morley experiment]] (1887) to disprove the existence of the [[luminiferous aether]]. Today used in [[Fourier transform spectroscopy]] and [[gravitational wave detector]]s.

== Coherence ==
{{Main|Coherence (physics)}}
Interference requires [[coherence (physics)|coherence]]:
* '''Temporal coherence''' — relates to monochromaticity (line width)
* '''Spatial coherence''' — relates to source size

[[Laser]]s are highly coherent; conventional light sources are partially coherent.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Diffraction]]
* [[Superposition principle]]
* [[Standing wave]]
* [[Beat (acoustics)]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Interference| ]]
[[Category:Optics]]
[[Category:Wave mechanics]]
